  The five trustworthy-QA axes are: accuracy (exact-match), citation faithfulness,
  abstain recall (correctly withholding on held-out unknowns), grounding-AUROC
  (structural score separates known from unknown), and novelty-F1 (detects
  out-of-memory queries).  The \emph{SMA vs Dense} column shows
  $\Delta = \text{SMA} - \text{Dense}$ with 95\% bootstrap CI and Holm-adjusted
  $p$-values from \texttt{qa\_stats.csv}.
  n/a = metric not applicable (closed-book has no retrieval scores).}

% ============================================================
% ED TABLE 3 — Synthetic Structure Benchmark (SSB): structure-only control
% Source: reports/confirmatory/ssb_stats.csv, ssb_summary.csv
% ============================================================

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Table~3 $|$ Synthetic Structure Benchmark (SSB): a
    structure-only control.}
  Zero-lexical-overlap, lattice-bridged relational analogs where the gold match is
  recoverable only by structure-mapping; BM25 and TF-IDF/dense retrieval are
  information-theoretically blind by construction. \emph{Library} task: $n{=}200$ pooled
  queries ($100$ queries $\times\,2$ seeds, seeds $41/43$); \emph{forced-choice}: each query
  against a single structural distractor. Deltas, 95\% CIs, Holm-adjusted $p$-values, and
  Cliff's $\delta$ are SMA versus each baseline (paired bootstrap). The control is
  de-circularized (no \texttt{far\_}-prefix bijection; no $(i,i{+}2)$ rewiring).
  All numbers from \texttt{reports/confirmatory/ssb\_stats.csv} and \texttt{ssb\_summary.csv}.}
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\begin{tabular}{llcccccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Task} & \textbf{Method} & \textbf{$r@1$} & \textbf{MRR} &
    \textbf{$\Delta\,r@1$} & \textbf{95\% CI} & \textbf{$p_{\mathrm{Holm}}$} & \textbf{Cliff's $\delta$} \\
\midrule
\multirow{3}{*}{Library}
  & \textbf{SMA (ours)} & \textbf{0.895} & \textbf{0.948} & --- & --- & --- & --- \\
  & BM25                & 0.000 & 0.010 & $+0.895$ & $[0.850, 0.935]$ & $4{\times}10^{-4}$ & $0.895$ \\
  & TF-IDF/dense        & 0.000 & 0.010 & $+0.895$ & $[0.850, 0.935]$ & $4{\times}10^{-4}$ & $0.895$ \\
\midrule
Forced-choice
  & \textbf{SMA (ours)} & \textbf{1.000} & \textbf{1.000} & --- & --- & --- & --- \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{flushleft}
{\footnotesize
$r@1$ = rank-1 accuracy; MRR = mean reciprocal rank. BM25 and TF-IDF/dense score $0.000$ at
rank-1 (MRR $\approx 0.010$, at chance) because query and gold entry share no surface tokens;
the match is recoverable only by aligning higher-order relational structure across the
ascension lattice. SSB carries no information-content gradient for either baseline to exploit,
so the result isolates SMA's structure-mapping mechanism from both lexical overlap and
information-content similarity.}
\end{flushleft}
\label{tab:ed3_ssb}
\end{table}


% ============================================================
% ED TABLE 4 — Ancestor-expanded RAG + IC-similarity fair baselines
% Source: reports/confirmatory/ontology_fair_baselines.csv
% ============================================================

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Table~4 $|$ Ancestor-expanded RAG and
    information-content baselines (rare-slice top-5).}
  Each baseline indexes the byte-identical flattened term-name record; the
  $+$ancestors arms additionally expand every document and query term-set with
  its full is-a ancestor closure before indexing. Phenomizer (IC best-match
  average) and Jaccard are ontology-aware information-content references.
  $\Delta = \text{SMA} - \text{method}$ on the rare-query slice; $p_{\mathrm{Holm}}$
  is Holm--Bonferroni-corrected across baselines within each ontology
  ($10^4$ paired-bootstrap resamples). Giving BM25/dense the is-a ancestor
  closure \emph{lowers} their accuracy (common ancestors dilute IDF/cosine);
  SMA beats every expanded RAG arm. The sole non-significant contrast is plain
  BM25 on GO. All numbers from
  \texttt{reports/confirmatory/ontology\_fair\_baselines.csv}.}

\setlength{\tabcolsep}{6pt}

& \multicolumn{3}{c}{\textbf{HPO (rare disease)}} & \multicolumn{3}{c}{\textbf{GO (gene function)}} \\

\textbf{Method} & \textbf{Top-5} & \textbf{$\Delta$} & \textbf{$p_{\mathrm{Holm}}$}
                & \textbf{Top-5} & \textbf{$\Delta$} & \textbf{$p_{\mathrm{Holm}}$} \\
\midrule
\textbf{SMA (ours)}        & \textbf{0.917} & ---      & ---     & \textbf{0.846} & ---      & ---     \\
\midrule
Phenomizer (IC-BMA)        & 0.952 & $-0.035$ & 0.047   & 0.860 & $-0.013$ & 0.677   \\
Jaccard                    & 0.708 & $+0.208$ & 0.0012  & 0.737 & $+0.110$ & 0.0012  \\
BM25                       & 0.731 & $+0.186$ & 0.0012  & 0.776 & $+0.070$ & 0.076   \\
BM25 $+$ ancestors         & 0.715 & $+0.202$ & 0.0012  & 0.504 & $+0.342$ & 0.0012  \\
Dense-RAG (TF-IDF)         & 0.622 & $+0.295$ & 0.0012  & 0.719 & $+0.127$ & 0.0012  \\

control: ancestor-expanding the retrievable text \emph{reduces} BM25 and dense
accuracy on both ontologies, so SMA's margin is not an artifact of ancestor
visibility. The only non-significant SMA contrast is against unexpanded BM25 on

\end{flushleft}
\label{tab:ed4_fair_baselines}
\end{table}


% ============================================================
% ED TABLE 5 — Elliptic Bitcoin graph-fraud boundary probe (honest null)

% ============================================================

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Table~5 $|$ Elliptic Bitcoin graph-fraud boundary
    probe (honest null).}
  SMA, dense, and lexical retrieval over a graph-neighbourhood licit/illicit
  typology encoding, versus a flat logistic-regression baseline on the full
  $166$-feature record. SMA shows no edge over the best retrieval baseline
  ($\Delta$ accuracy $+0.004$, 95\% CI $[-0.010,+0.019]$, $p{=}0.56$), and
  \emph{all} retrieval methods trail logistic regression. Macro-F1 averages over
  the licit and illicit classes; illicit-F1 isolates the rare fraud class
  ($219$ illicit of $2{,}250$ pooled test instances); ROC-AUC is threshold-free.
  All numbers from \texttt{reports/confirmatory/agentic\_fraud\_elliptic.csv}
  ($k{=}15$; seeds $7|17|23$).}
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{8pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Method} & \textbf{Macro-F1} & \textbf{Illicit-F1} & \textbf{ROC-AUC} \\
\midrule
SMA (graph typology)        & 0.581 & 0.243 & 0.622 \\
Dense-RAG                   & 0.586 & 0.262 & 0.727 \\
BM25                        & 0.602 & 0.289 & 0.709 \\
\midrule

\begin{flushleft}
{\footnotesize
\textbf{Bold} $=$ best per column. The flat logistic-regression baseline on the
native $166$ engineered transaction features outperforms every
structure/retrieval method, including SMA, on all three metrics: on this task the
discriminative signal is in the per-record feature vector, not in a coarse
typology lattice over the transaction graph. SMA vs.\ best retrieval baseline:
$\Delta$ accuracy $+0.004$, 95\% CI $[-0.010,+0.019]$, $p{=}0.56$
(per-query paired bootstrap; the three seeds share entities, so the effective $N$
is $\approx 3\times$ smaller than the $2{,}250$ pooled rows).
Source: \texttt{reports/confirmatory/agentic\_fraud\_elliptic.csv}.}
\end{flushleft}
\label{tab:ed5_elliptic}
\end{table}


% ============================================================
% ED TABLE 6 — Benchmark datasheet (Gebru-style) for the memory-swap benchmark
% Sources: sma/eval/agentic/arms/*.py, harness.py, attack.py,
%          reports/confirmatory/agentic_{domain}.csv
% ============================================================

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Table~6 $|$ Datasheet for the memory-swap
    benchmark} (Gebru-style). Per-arm data source, licence, eligibility band, and
  pooled query counts; the shared query generator, indexing, and fairness
  protocol are stated in the notes. Sources are version-pinned; all counts are
  from \texttt{reports/confirmatory/agentic\_\{domain\}.csv} and the arm loaders
  in \texttt{sma/eval/agentic/arms/}.}
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\begin{tabular}{lllccccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Arm} & \textbf{Entity $\to$ gold} & \textbf{Source} & \textbf{Licence} &
  \textbf{Band} & \textbf{$n_\text{all}$} & \textbf{$n_\text{rare}$} & \textbf{$n_\text{novel}$} \\
\midrule
Medicine & disease $\to$ HPO phenotypes      & \texttt{phenotype.hpoa} (HPO/OMIM/Orphanet) & CC BY 4.0$^{\ddagger}$ & 7--30  & 324 & 274 & 36 \\
Genomics & protein $\to$ GO processes         & \texttt{goa\_human.gaf} (GOA)              & CC BY 4.0              & 7--30  & 324 & 192 & 36 \\
Finance  & filing $\to$ US-GAAP concepts      & SEC FSDS \texttt{num.txt} (2024Q1)         & Public domain          & 10--40 & 324 & 208 & 36 \\
Cyber    & threat group $\to$ ATT\&CK TTPs    & ATT\&CK STIX \texttt{enterprise-attack.json} & Apache-2.0           & 7--30  & 222 & 175 & 24 \\
Legal/IP & patent $\to$ CPC subgroups         & USPTO grant XML \texttt{ipg161011.xml}     & Public domain          & 7--30  & 324 & \textit{n/a}$^{\dagger}$ & 36 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{flushleft}
{\footnotesize
\textbf{Eligibility.} An entity is eligible only if its gold term-set falls in the
arm's band (\texttt{MIN\_TERMS}--\texttt{MAX\_TERMS}): 7--30 for
medicine/genomics/cyber/legal, 10--40 for finance. Below the floor a case is
trivial; above the ceiling SME kernel enumeration becomes intractable (excluding
e.g.\ the most-documented threat groups). Bands are applied identically to all
memories.

\textbf{Query generator} (\texttt{harness.py:make\_qspec}, shared by every arm and
every memory). From an entity's eligible term-set: (i) sample at most $5$ gold
terms (\texttt{rng.sample(terms, min(5, len(terms)))}); (ii) for each kept term,
climb $0$--$2$ is-a levels by \texttt{rng.choice([0,0,1,1,2])} (replacing the term
with a random asserted parent at each step) to model imprecise observation;
(iii) append $3$ in-vocabulary noise terms drawn from the indexed pool
(\texttt{rng.sample(noise\_pool, 3)}). The resulting query carries no gold-entity
identifier --- only term ids, rendered to text via the identical term-name lookup
used at indexing.

\textbf{Indexing, splits, and seeds.} Per seed, the eligible pool is shuffled and
truncated to \texttt{n\_index}$=2000$; a \texttt{holdout\_frac}$=0.1$ slice is
reserved as held-out (NOVEL) entities (their true key is never indexed and feeds
the abstain/novelty metrics), the remainder is indexed. The rare/long-tail slice
is defined by information content (rarest gold term above the corpus median),
computed over the \emph{indexed} records only (no test-on-train leak). Counts are
pooled over $3$ seeds $\{7,17,23\}$ with $120$ queries per seed; reported
$n_\text{all}/n_\text{rare}/n_\text{novel}$ are these pooled totals (cyber is
smaller because few intrusion-sets meet the 7--30 band).

\textbf{Fairness (identical-record protocol).} Every memory --- SMA, BM25,
BGE-dense, hybrid-RRF, hybrid$+$rerank, HippoRAG --- indexes the \emph{byte-identical}
flattened term-name record built once per entity
(\texttt{IndexItem.text = " ".join(term\_name(t))}); only the retrieval mechanism
differs. Crucially, is-a ancestors are \emph{deliberately withheld} from the
baseline documents and queries: SMA reaches ancestors at match time through
bounded lattice ascension, whereas adding the ancestor closure to the baseline
text was tested directly and \emph{hurts} the baselines (common ancestors dilute
IDF and cosine; Extended Data Table~4), so withholding them
is the fair, not the favourable, choice.

\textbf{Cyber alias guard.} The mounted ATT\&CK graph contains \emph{only}
technique terms (keyed by external id, e.g.\ \texttt{T1059.001}) and tactic terms;
\texttt{intrusion-set} objects (threat-group names) and their
\texttt{x\_mitre\_aliases} are never materialized as terms
(\texttt{sma/ontology/attack.py}). Group names are used solely as gold entity
keys, so no threat-group name or alias can enter any indexed document or query
text --- the attribution task cannot be solved by name leakage.

$^{\dagger}$Legal: CPC's near-uniform information content yields no rare split
($n_\text{rare}=0$ of $324$); legal is scored on the all-query slice.
$^{\ddagger}$We redistribute derived gold labels (disease ids and phenotype
sets), not OMIM source records.}
\end{flushleft}
\label{tab:ed6_datasheet}
\end{table}

\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\begin{tabular}{lllc}
\toprule
\textbf{Source} & \textbf{Role} & \textbf{License} & \textbf{Redistributed} \\
\midrule
HPO~\cite{kohler2021hpo}            & Ontology (medicine)        & CC BY 4.0      & ontology \\
GO~\cite{ashburner2000go}           & Ontology (genomics)        & CC BY 4.0      & ontology \\
MONDO~\cite{vasilevsky2022mondo}    & Ontology (disease)         & CC BY 4.0      & ontology \\
Uberon                              & Ontology (anatomy)         & CC BY 4.0      & ontology \\
ChEBI                               & Ontology (chemistry)       & CC BY 4.0      & ontology \\
Orphanet                            & Gold annotations (medicine)& CC BY 4.0      & derived gold \\
GO Annotation File (GOA)            & Gold annotations (genomics)& CC BY 4.0      & derived gold \\
MITRE ATT\&CK~\cite{strom2018attack}& Ontology + gold (cyber)    & Apache-2.0     & ontology + gold \\
MITRE CAPEC / CWE                   & Ontology (cyber)           & public terms of use & ontology \\
CPC                                 & Ontology + gold (legal/IP) & public domain  & ontology + gold \\
US-GAAP                             & Ontology (finance)         & public domain  & ontology \\
FIBO                                & Ontology (finance)         & MIT            & ontology \\
LKIF-core                           & Ontology (legal)           & open (LGPL-compatible) & ontology \\
SEC EDGAR filings                   & Gold (finance)             & public domain  & derived gold \\
USPTO patent CPC assignments        & Gold (legal/IP)            & public domain  & derived gold \\
OMIM                                & Gold annotations (medicine)& restricted     & \textbf{derived only}$^\dagger$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{flushleft}
{\footnotesize
$^\dagger$\textbf{OMIM caveat.} We do not redistribute OMIM source records. For the
medicine arm we release only \emph{derived} gold labels --- disease identifiers
paired with their phenotype (HPO term) sets, obtained through the openly licensed
HPO disease--phenotype annotation file --- not OMIM clinical-synopsis text or any
OMIM source field. Users requiring OMIM source records must obtain them directly
from OMIM under its own terms.
SNOMED CT and UMLS are deliberately excluded in favour of fully open
reproducibility (see Limitations).}
\end{flushleft}
\label{tab:ed7_licenses}
\end{table}


% ============================================================
% STATISTICAL-RIGOR ED SUBSECTIONS + ED TABLE 8 (vs-all-baselines)
% Source: reports/confirmatory/agentic_vs_all_baselines.csv
% ============================================================

\subsection*{Leakage regime: out-of-index, in-ontology}

Held-out cases are \emph{out-of-index} (the entity's case record is removed
from the indexed pool) but \emph{in-ontology} (its term classes remain in the
mounted lattice). Unknown and known cases therefore draw from the same
vocabulary---the harder, more realistic regime---rather than the trivial
setting in which novelty coincides with unseen terms. Novelty is detected from
\emph{expectation violation} against the indexed cases (the structural
grounding score has no analogue to align to), not from term absence. The
rare-by-information-content threshold (rarest-term IC above the corpus median)
is computed over the \emph{indexed pool only}, so the slice definition never
consults held-out cases and no test-on-train leakage can inflate the rare-slice
contrast.

\subsection*{Statistical rigor: multiplicity, resampling units, and selection}

\textit{Two Holm families.} We apply Holm--Bonferroni correction within two
pre-registered families: the \emph{retrieval-suite} family (across the five
evaluated domains: medicine, genomics, finance, cyber, legal) and the
\emph{end-to-end} family (across the five trustworthy-QA axes: accuracy,
abstain-recall, novelty-recall, selective-accuracy, grounding-AUROC).

\textit{Resampling unit.} The retrieval-suite paired bootstrap ($10^4$
resamples, seed 12345) is \emph{per-query, not per-entity}: the three seeds
share the same underlying entities, so the effective number of independent
entities is roughly one-third of the resampled query count. We disclose this
because per-query resampling treats correlated queries over a shared entity as
independent and therefore yields anti-conservative confidence intervals; the
end-to-end design avoids this (its $120$ answerable and $120$ held-out cases are
distinct diseases, one query each, and the bootstrap resamples entities, with
the grounding-AUROC bootstrap resampling the positive and negative pools
separately).

\textit{Selection correction (D5).} The headline comparator ``best RAG'' is
\emph{data-selected}: the strongest baseline is chosen by all-query top-5 and
then tested on the same statistic, which double-dips. To address this directly
we compare SMA against \emph{every} one of the five baselines in \emph{every}
domain (\texttt{agentic\_vs\_all\_baselines.csv}). SMA's tail top-5 advantage is
strictly positive against all five baselines in all five domains, so the
``best-RAG'' win is not a selection artifact. Applying a conservative
Bonferroni-over-baselines correction ($\times 5$) to the vs-best $p$-value,
\emph{four of five domains survive}: medicine, genomics and finance at
$p_\text{Bonf}=0.0010$ and legal at $p_\text{Bonf}=0.0110$. Cyber does not
survive the selection correction ($p=0.035 \to p_\text{Bonf}=0.17$) and we
report it as \emph{directional}, consistent with threat groups' distinctive
technique vocabularies, which lexical and dense retrieval partly capture.

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Table~8 $|$ SMA versus \emph{every} retrieval
    baseline, with selection-corrected significance.}
  Tail top-5 (rare slice; legal on the all-query slice) advantage
  $\Delta=\text{SMA}-\text{baseline}$ for each of the five baselines in each
  domain; all $\Delta>0$. The right block reports the data-selected ``best RAG''
  comparator (chosen by all-query top-5), its raw paired-bootstrap $p$, and the
  Bonferroni-over-baselines ($\times 5$) corrected $p_\text{Bonf}$.
  Source: \texttt{reports/confirmatory/agentic\_vs\_all\_baselines.csv}.}
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccccc cc c}
\toprule
 & \multicolumn{5}{c}{\textbf{$\Delta$ tail top-5 vs.\ each baseline}}
   & \multicolumn{3}{c}{\textbf{vs.\ best (selected)}} \\
\cmidrule(lr){2-6}\cmidrule(lr){7-9}
\textbf{Domain} & BM25 & Dense & Hyb-RRF & Hyb+Rr & HippoRAG
  & $p_\text{raw}$ & $p_\text{Bonf}$ & Survives \\
\midrule
Medicine & $+0.46$ & $+0.45$ & $+0.44$ & $+0.34$ & $+0.59$ & $0.0002$ & $0.0010$ & yes \\
Genomics & $+0.30$ & $+0.17$ & $+0.15$ & $+0.20$ & $+0.53$ & $0.0002$ & $0.0010$ & yes \\
Finance  & $+0.17$ & $+0.23$ & $+0.19$ & $+0.22$ & $+0.32$ & $0.0002$ & $0.0010$ & yes \\
Legal$^\dagger$ & $+0.27$ & $+0.07$ & $+0.15$ & $+0.15$ & $+0.52$ & $0.0022$ & $0.0110$ & yes \\
Cyber    & $+0.06$ & $+0.14$ & $+0.02$ & $+0.11$ & $+0.39$ & $0.0346$ & $0.1730$ & \textbf{no} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{flushleft}
{\footnotesize
$\Delta$ values are the marginal SMA tail top-5 (rare slice) minus each
baseline's tail top-5; legal$^\dagger$ uses the all-query slice (CPC has no rare
split). These per-baseline marginal differences are distinct from---and for the
narrow cyber margin smaller than---the per-query paired-bootstrap mean $\Delta$
reported against the committed best baseline in Table~2. The
``vs.\ best'' columns correct the data-selected comparator by Bonferroni over
the five baselines. Four of five domains survive ($p_\text{Bonf}\le 0.011$);
cyber is directional ($p=0.035$, $p_\text{Bonf}=0.17$). $p_\text{raw}=0.0002$ is
the paired-bootstrap floor ($1/(R{+}1)$, $R=10^4$). The bootstrap is per-query,
not per-entity (three seeds share entities), so reported $p$-values are
anti-conservative at the entity level.}
\end{flushleft}
\label{tab:ed8_vs_all_baselines}
\end{table}


% ============================================================
% ED TABLE 9 — Mechanism ablation: lexical -> +is-a -> +IC -> +typed
% Source: reports/confirmatory/ablation_mechanism.csv
% 3 seeds, n_index=400, n_query=90, rare-slice tail top-5,
% paired bootstrap of adjacent rungs.
% ============================================================

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Table~9 $|$ Mechanism ablation: what
    structure-mapping adds, one component at a time.}
  Starting from a lexical-only (BM25) base, we add the is-a ascension lattice,
  then information-content (surprisal) weighting, then higher-order typed
  relations, and report rare-slice tail top-5 accuracy at each rung.
  $\Delta$ is the paired-bootstrap difference against the immediately preceding
  rung (3 seeds, $n_\text{index}{=}400$, $n_\text{query}{=}90$). Medicine (HPO)
  is a pure-is-a control with no typed relations to add; genomics (GO) carries
  typed relations. The is-a rung is the dominant lever (HPO $+0.368$,
  GO $+0.080$); IC weighting adds a significant second increment; typed
  higher-order relations add no measurable top-5 accuracy.
  All numbers from \texttt{reports/confirmatory/ablation\_mechanism.csv}.}
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{6pt}
\begin{tabular}{llcccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Domain} & \textbf{Rung (cumulative mechanism)} & \textbf{Top-5 (rare)} &
    \textbf{$\Delta$ vs.\ prev} & \textbf{95\% CI} & \textbf{$p$} \\
\midrule
\multirow{4}{*}{Genomics (GO)}
  & lexical-only (BM25)              & 0.750 & ---        & ---                  & ---     \\
  & $+$ is-a ascension              & 0.830 & $+0.080$   & $[-0.005,+0.160]$    & 0.066   \\
  & $+$ IC weighting                & 0.910 & $+0.080$   & $[+0.047,+0.118]$    & $2{\times}10^{-4}$ \\
  & $+$ higher-order / typed        & 0.915 & $+0.005$   & $[+0.000,+0.014]$    & 0.72 (n.s.) \\
\midrule
\multirow{4}{*}{\shortstack[l]{Medicine (HPO,\\pure-is-a control)}}
  & lexical-only (BM25)              & 0.587 & ---        & ---                  & ---     \\
  & $+$ is-a ascension              & 0.955 & $+0.368$   & $[+0.296,+0.440]$    & $2{\times}10^{-4}$ \\
  & $+$ IC weighting                & 0.982 & $+0.027$   & $[+0.009,+0.049]$    & 0.003   \\
  & $+$ higher-order / typed        & 0.982 & $+0.000$   & $[+0.000,+0.000]$    & ---     \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{flushleft}
{\footnotesize
Rungs are cumulative: each row adds one mechanism to the row above. $\Delta$ is
the paired-bootstrap difference of adjacent rungs (rare-slice tail top-5, top-5
tail). HPO is a pure-is-a ontology, so its $+$higher-order rung adds no typed
relations and yields $\Delta{=}{+}0.000$ by construction. The advantage
decomposes into is-a subsumption (the dominant lever) plus information-content
weighting (a significant second increment); typed higher-order relations add no
measurable top-5 accuracy (GO $+0.005$, n.s.), consistent with the Phenomizer IC
parity (Extended Data Table~4) and the SSB structure-only control (Extended Data
Table~3). The ablation runs at a reduced index scale ($n_\text{index}{=}400$; the
full-scale run is compute-bound), so the robust quantity is the \emph{relative}
contribution of each rung rather than the absolute accuracies.}
\end{flushleft}
\label{tab:ed9_mechanism_ablation}
\end{table}


% ============================================================
% ED FIGURE 1 — Per-domain AURC + novelty-F1 across all 6 memories
% ============================================================

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../figures/svg/ed_domain_aurc}
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Fig.~1 $|$ Per-domain AURC and Novelty-F1 across
    all six memory systems.}
  \textbf{a},~AURC (area under the risk-coverage curve; lower is better):
  SMA achieves the lowest AURC in all 5 of 5 domains, indicating superior
  selective abstention — it correctly withholds on hard or novel queries.
  Finance is the hardest domain for all methods (sparse ontology coverage).
  \textbf{b},~Novelty-F1: only SMA and HippoRAG return non-zero novelty-F1;
  the four lexical/dense baselines cannot distinguish known from unknown at all
  (novelty-F1 = 0).  SMA novelty-F1 ranges from 0.177 (Genomics) to 0.182
  (Medicine, Finance, Legal), reflecting the fixed 36-novel-query injection
  across arms.
  Source: \texttt{reports/confirmatory/agentic\_\{domain\}.csv}.}
\label{fig:ed1_domain_aurc}
\end{figure}


% ============================================================
% ED FIGURE 2 — Phase 5 risk-coverage / selective-prediction curve
% ============================================================

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.65\textwidth]{../figures/svg/ed_risk_coverage}
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Fig.~2 $|$ Phase~5 selective-prediction
    (risk-coverage) curve.}
  Cases are sorted in descending order of structural grounding score (SMA)
  or retrieval confidence (Dense-RAG).  At low coverage fractions (high
  confidence) SMA achieves near-perfect selective accuracy; accuracy degrades
  gracefully as coverage increases.  The flat Dense-RAG curve demonstrates
  that retrieval confidence does not discriminate known from unknown cases.
  AURC (SMA) = 0.6253 vs Dense-RAG = 0.8248 (lower = better).
  Source: \texttt{reports/confirmatory/qa\_sma.csv},
          \texttt{qa\_dense.csv}, \texttt{qa\_sma\_summary.csv}.}
\label{fig:ed2_risk_coverage}
\end{figure}


% ============================================================
% ED FIGURE 3 — Paired cross-system transfer comparison (per-leg)
% ============================================================

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../figures/svg/ed_transfer}
\caption*{\textbf{Extended Data Fig.~3 $|$ Paired cross-system transfer, per leg
    (seed~201).}
  macro-F1 for each transfer leg of the LogHub benchmark
  (\texttt{t1\_transfer\_metrics.csv}), comparing SMA against all baselines
  without leg-averaging (which masks per-leg imbalance).
  BGL\,$\to$\,Thunderbird: SMA achieves 0.715 macro-F1.
  BGL\,$\to$\,Spirit: SMA is competitive but one seed shows large variance
  (seeds 201–205 are shown in aggregate stats in \texttt{t1\_stats.csv}).
  HDFS\,$\to$\,OpenStack: all methods collapse to 0.333 (retrieval degeneracy;
  three-class uniform prediction; documented diagnostic in the CSV).
  The honest null on HDFS\,$\to$\,OpenStack is reported and does not affect
  the main claims.
  Source: \texttt{reports/confirmatory/t1\_transfer\_metrics.csv}.}
\label{fig:ed3_transfer}
\end{figure}
